h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I've got mine going the wrong way.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Wait — had it right the first time.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner appears headed toward erasing and correcting the fox-rabbit arrow because they believe it points the wrong way.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: — that one eats grass — [arrow down] so the whole bottom layer works like this, every plant box fans out to everything that eats it— [glances up at remaining boxes] which box is the tail for fox?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner uses the eater criterion to place the second point and generalizes the fan-out structure, though they still need help transferring the tail rule to fox.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: L2's false closure about energy moving in one line triggers T2 to contrast the failed linear route with a new fork/tail/point mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise, economical, and fitted to the learner's blockage because it preserves the food-to-eater rule while giving a usable representation for branching.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor takes up the learner's revised but overextended framing and changes the representation from a line to a fork.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange moves forward from an overgeneralized label toward sorting length and area ratios correctly.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence:
Side and perimeter under k. Area under k squared.
Rule: if the side factor is k, the area factor is k squared — that's what both cases give.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new classification and generalizes it into the k versus k squared rule.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence:
Not a different operation. A different quantity being compared.

Side length compares \(4\) cm with \(2\) cm. Put that under length ratios.

Perimeter is still distance, not surface. Which column takes it?

[The tutor pauses, then brackets the two area counts: \(4\text{ cm}^2\) and \(16\text{ cm}^2\).]

Area cannot be carried by that same label. It compares square centimetres: \(16 \div 4 = 4\).

So the label must name its object: length scale factor \(2\), perimeter scale factor \(2\), area scale factor \(4\). If the side factor were \(3\), which comparison would give the area label?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: L2 creates pressure by exposing uncertainty about whether area scale factor is a different kind of label, and T2 responds by changing the task into a two-column quantity-sorting mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence:
Not a different operation. A different quantity being compared.

Side length compares \(4\) cm with \(2\) cm. Put that under length ratios.

Perimeter is still distance, not surface. Which column takes it?

[The tutor pauses, then brackets the two area counts: \(4\text{ cm}^2\) and \(16\text{ cm}^2\).]

Area cannot be carried by that same label. It compares square centimetres: \(16 \div 4 = 4\).

So the label must name its object: length scale factor \(2\), perimeter scale factor \(2\), area scale factor \(4\). If the side factor were \(3\), which comparison would give the area label?
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is specific, economical, and fitted to the blockage because it separates operation from compared quantity and gives the learner a reusable column criterion.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no recognitive learner pivot, so no later tutor uptake of a revised learner self-framing can be scored.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The shortage doesn't come from nowhere.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: All right — if the pumps were dry, then stock orders.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected either to keep collapsing pain and cause or simply accept the dry-pump check as a narrow first test.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: if the pumps were dry, then stock orders. Because whoever signed off on what they ordered knew the margin they were sitting on.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner applies the new evidence route by selecting stock orders as the next demand under the supply-chain charge.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence:
That sentence stays. It does not clear the company. It stops the branch treating one fact as two facts.

[draws two boxes on the board: PUMP GOUGING / SUPPLY CONSTRAINT.]

Put one fact in one box.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L1 resists the cause distinction, T2 takes stock of the learner's political objection and introduces a two-box sorting mechanism rather than merely repeating the initial question.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence:
Put one fact in one box.

“All stations put twelve percent on, including stations with full tanks and no queues.”

That is evidence for gouging at the pump.

“Co-op pumps were dry before the rise, while nearby full stations did not move by twelve.”

That is evidence for constraint showing through the price.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is specific, economical, and fitted to the learner's blockage because it preserves blame while giving a usable evidence classification.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no later tutor turn after the L3 pivot.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no later tutor turn after the L3 pivot.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: After L1 resists the cause distinction, T2 takes stock of the learner's political objection and introduces a two-box sorting mechanism rather than merely repeating the initial question.

scored.recoheredEarlier: Everything's ticked — all twelve points. The first-year group collects it tomorrow morning, so the form should be ready to sign.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: So — the form being complete isn't the same as... right.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange was moving toward locating the needed power-on evidence and writing a one-line stop rule from the sheet and bench.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: The polarity mark — that would be the visible thing, before anything goes on. But the power-on line — [quieter] — it doesn't seem to say *if the mark is wrong or missing, stop*. Maybe there's a line earlier that covers it and — [pause] — is that condition somewhere here that I'm not finding, or is it actually not written?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner applies the stop-condition evidence standard to the actual power-on line and discovers that the required action gate is missing or unfindable.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The failed search now counts as bench evidence. If the stop condition sits elsewhere, this line must point to it; if no such line can be found, the power-on tick is not a release condition. Polarity visible and correct, or the supply remains off and supervision is called. Until that can be read at the action point and shown on the cold board, the signature waits.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T4 converts the learner's failed search into an evidence standard and changes the task from finding reassurance to deciding whether the tick can function as a release condition.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: The failed search now counts as bench evidence. If the stop condition sits elsewhere, this line must point to it; if no such line can be found, the power-on tick is not a release condition. Polarity visible and correct, or the supply remains off and supervision is called.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise, economical, and fitted to the blockage: absence at the action point becomes usable evidence, with a clear stop rule the learner can apply.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The failed search now counts as bench evidence. If the stop condition sits elsewhere, this line must point to it; if no such line can be found, the power-on tick is not a release condition.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor directly takes up the learner's revised frame of missing or unfindable evidence and turns it into the governing criterion for action.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner is being guided from naming the interacting object toward placing the friction tile by contact evidence.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence:
[places one finger on the bottom edge of the cart drawing and holds it there, eyes moving to the arrows]

[reaches for the friction tile and sets it on the arrow meeting that edge]
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner uses the new contact-surface criterion to place the friction tile rather than merely naming the force or declaring understanding.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Floor names the object. Put one finger on the cart surface the floor touches. Keep it there. Point to the arrow for that contact acting on the cart; then move the friction tile.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: L2 shows hesitation and motion-arrow confusion, and T3 introduces a useful finger-on-contact procedure, but it does not explicitly take stock of a failed route.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Put one finger on the cart surface the floor touches. Keep it there. Point to the arrow for that contact acting on the cart; then move the friction tile.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise, economical, and fitted to the blockage because it converts the learner's vague arrow-choice problem into a usable contact-evidence standard.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no recognition pivot, so there is no later tutor uptake of a revised learner framing.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no recognition pivot, so there is no later tutor uptake of a revised learner framing.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: L2 shows hesitation and motion-arrow confusion, and T3 introduces a useful finger-on-contact procedure, but it does not explicitly take stock of a failed route.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh — so just that one entry.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner is expected to identify whether labelled cells are headers or row values by using placement on the sheet.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: That one's inside one of the rows, not at the top — so that's... row value?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner applies the placement criterion to a new labelled cell and produces the classification despite remaining uncertainty.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: That placement is the reason. `rainfall_mm` is above the rows, so it names the field: `header`, not `row value`. Now use placement on the next labelled cell before the wording: above the rows means field/header; inside one row means row value. Which side of that line is the next labelled cell on?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor responds to uncertainty by foregrounding a criterion and applying it to the next cell, but this is a clearer continuation of the same route rather than a stock-taking reversal with a new public mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Now use placement on the next labelled cell before the wording: above the rows means field/header; inside one row means row value. Which side of that line is the next labelled cell on?
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is usable and fitted to the learner's uncertainty, but it is mostly a sharpened version of the existing placement test rather than a visibly different adaptive device.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no recognitive learner pivot, so there is no later tutor uptake of a revised learner framing.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no recognitive learner pivot, so there is no later tutor uptake of a revised learner framing.
